```go
1	// Command wanbond is the wanbond tunnel daemon. A single binary serves both
2	// roles (edge and concentrator); the role is selected from configuration at
3	// runtime, not by which binary is invoked.
4	package main
5	
6	import (
7		"flag"
8		"fmt"
9		"os"
10		"os/signal"
11		"syscall"
12	
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
14		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/device"
15		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
16	)
17	
18	// version is overridden at build time via -ldflags "-X main.version=...".
19	var version = "dev"
20	
21	func main() {
22		if err := run(os.Args[1:]); err != nil {
23			fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, "wanbond:", err)
24			os.Exit(1)
25		}
26	}
27	
28	// run is the testable entry point: it parses the command line, loads the
29	// configuration, brings the tunnel up for the configured role, and blocks until
30	// a termination signal, then tears the tunnel down.
31	func run(args []string) error {
32		if len(args) == 1 && (args[0] == "version" || args[0] == "--version") {
33			fmt.Println("wanbond", version)
34			return nil
35		}
36	
37		fs := flag.NewFlagSet("wanbond", flag.ContinueOnError)
38		configPath := fs.String("config", "", "path to the TOML configuration file (mode 0600)")
39		if err := fs.Parse(args); err != nil {
40			return err
41		}
42		if *configPath == "" {
43			return fmt.Errorf("--config is required (or `wanbond version`)")
44		}
45	
46		cfg, err := config.Load(*configPath)
47		if err != nil {
48			return err
49		}
50	
51		lg, err := log.New(cfg.Log.Level, os.Stderr)
52		if err != nil {
53			return err
54		}
55		main := lg.Component("main")
56		main.Info("wanbond starting", "version", version, "role", string(cfg.Role))
57		warnUnverifiableWeightedCapacity(main, cfg)
58	
59		tun, err := device.Up(cfg, lg)
60		if err != nil {
61			return err
62		}
63		defer tun.Close()
64	
65		main.Info("tunnel interface up", "interface", tun.Name(), "role", string(cfg.Role))
66	
67		// SIGINT/SIGTERM terminate; SIGHUP reloads the config file to add/remove paths
68		// on the running tunnel (T30). SIGHUP is idiomatic for a daemon config reload and
69		// needs no extra socket or privilege.
70		sig := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
71		signal.Notify(sig, syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM, syscall.SIGHUP)
72		stopped := deviceStopped(tun)
73		for {
74			select {
75			case s := <-sig:
76				if s == syscall.SIGHUP {
77					reloadTunnel(main, tun, *configPath)
78					continue
79				}
80				main.Info("shutting down", "signal", s.String())
81				return nil
82			case <-stopped:
83				// Unrecoverable engine teardown: fail loud so the exit status reflects it
84				// and a supervisor (systemd Restart=on-failure) restarts the daemon.
85				return fmt.Errorf("tunnel device stopped unexpectedly")
86			}
87		}
88	}
89	
90	// warnUnverifiableWeightedCapacity logs the Q52 WARN-arm startup diagnostic (T144)
91	// when cfg's weighted-policy capacity-sanity verdict is UNVERIFIABLE — some or all
92	// paths leave link_bandwidth undeclared, so the aggregate capacity accounting cannot
93	// be checked. It is a NO-OP (not applicable, no WARN) when cfg.WeightedCapacitySane
94	// is nil (the active-backup policy) or SANE-VERIFIED (every path declared). This is
95	// the soft, non-blocking complement to config.Load's T142 hard-fail guard: startup
96	// must NEVER be blocked on an unverifiable declaration, only on a DECLARED path that
97	// actively contradicts the guard (which Load already refused before main ever runs).
98	func warnUnverifiableWeightedCapacity(lg log.Logger, cfg *config.Config) {
99		if cfg.WeightedCapacitySane == nil || *cfg.WeightedCapacitySane {
100			return
101		}
102		declared := 0
103		for _, p := range cfg.Paths {
104			if p.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec > 0 {
105				declared++
106			}
107		}
108		lg.Warn("weighted policy: link_bandwidth capacity is unverifiable",
109			"declared_paths", declared, "total_paths", len(cfg.Paths),
110			"remedy", "declare link_bandwidth on ALL paths so capacity can be checked, or verify per_path_capacity_fps against the BDP sizing in install.md §3a")
111	}
112	
113	// reloadTunnel re-reads and validates the config file and applies its path diff to
114	// the running tunnel. A bad reload (unreadable, insecure-mode, invalid, or a failed
115	// path change) is LOGGED and the running tunnel is left intact — a reload must never
116	// tear down a live session. config.Load performs the fail-fast validation.
117	func reloadTunnel(lg log.Logger, tun *device.Tunnel, configPath string) {
118		cfg, err := config.Load(configPath)
119		if err != nil {
120			lg.Error("config reload rejected; keeping running config", "error", err.Error())
121			return
122		}
123		if err := tun.Reload(cfg); err != nil {
124			lg.Error("config reload failed to apply; tunnel still running", "error", err.Error())
125			return
126		}
127		lg.Info("config reloaded")
128	}
129	
130	// deviceStopped returns a channel that closes when the tunnel device tears down
131	// on its own (an unrecoverable engine error), so run can exit rather than block
132	// on a dead device.
133	func deviceStopped(t *device.Tunnel) <-chan struct{} {
134		done := make(chan struct{})
135		go func() {
136			t.Wait()
137			close(done)
138		}()
139		return done
140	}
```

```go
1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"fmt"
6		"sync"
7		"syscall"
8		"time"
9	
10		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun"
11	
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
13	)
14	
15	// tunDiagnosticInterval is the minimum spacing between actionable EIO diagnostics for a
16	// single TUN. An interface left DOWN (or otherwise wedged) fails EVERY subsequent write, so
17	// without a rate limit a write storm would emit one diagnostic record per dropped packet.
18	// The raw amneziawg-go engine error ("Failed to write packets to TUN device: input/output
19	// error", logged unconditionally via engineLogger's Errorf) still surfaces on every failure
20	// underneath — this decorator adds ONE extra, actionable record per interval on top of that,
21	// it never suppresses the engine's own logging.
22	const tunDiagnosticInterval = 30 * time.Second
23	
24	// diagnosingTUN wraps a tun.Device and, on a Write that fails with EIO, emits ONE rate-limited
25	// actionable ERROR naming the probable cause (the interface's link state and MTU) and the
26	// remedy, alongside the raw error for debugging (D39, I3). Left undiagnosed, a write to a DOWN
27	// wanbond0 surfaces only as the engine's generic "input/output error" — accurate but useless to
28	// an operator who has never hit the failure mode before. Every OTHER tun.Device method passes
29	// straight through via the embedded interface; only Write is intercepted.
30	type diagnosingTUN struct {
31		tun.Device
32		name string
33		log  log.Logger
34		// now and probeState are seams for tests: production wires time.Now and ifState (the real
35		// SIOCGIFFLAGS/SIOCGIFMTU inspection); a test injects a fake clock and a scripted interface
36		// state so it can assert the DOWN/UP wording without a real, privileged network interface.
37		now        func() time.Time
38		probeState func(name string) (up bool, mtu int, err error)
39	
40		mu       sync.Mutex
41		lastDiag time.Time
42	}
43	
44	// newDiagnosingTUN wraps dev for production use: real wall-clock rate-limiting and the real
45	// (platform-specific) interface-state inspection.
46	func newDiagnosingTUN(dev tun.Device, name string, lg log.Logger) *diagnosingTUN {
47		return &diagnosingTUN{Device: dev, name: name, log: lg, now: time.Now, probeState: ifState}
48	}
49	
50	// Write delegates to the wrapped Device and, on an EIO failure, reports a rate-limited
51	// actionable diagnostic. The original error is returned UNCHANGED: this decorator only adds a
52	// diagnostic side effect, it never masks or alters the engine's own error handling.
53	func (d *diagnosingTUN) Write(bufs [][]byte, offset int) (int, error) {
54		n, err := d.Device.Write(bufs, offset)
55		if err != nil && errors.Is(err, syscall.EIO) {
56			d.diagnoseEIO(err)
57		}
58		return n, err
59	}
60	
61	// diagnoseEIO inspects the interface state and logs ONE actionable ERROR record, then
62	// suppresses further diagnostics for tunDiagnosticInterval so a write storm (every dropped
63	// packet failing until the operator acts) produces one record, not a flood.
64	func (d *diagnosingTUN) diagnoseEIO(writeErr error) {
65		d.mu.Lock()
66		now := d.now()
67		if !d.lastDiag.IsZero() && now.Sub(d.lastDiag) < tunDiagnosticInterval {
68			d.mu.Unlock()
69			return
70		}
71		d.lastDiag = now
72		d.mu.Unlock()
73	
74		var errno syscall.Errno
75		_ = errors.As(writeErr, &errno)
76	
77		up, mtu, stateErr := d.probeState(d.name)
78		switch {
79		case stateErr != nil:
80			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
81				"errno", int(errno), "state", "unknown",
82				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("could not inspect %s state (%v) — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name, stateErr))
83		case !up:
84			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
85				"errno", int(errno), "state", "DOWN", "mtu", mtu,
86				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name))
87		default:
88			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
89				"errno", int(errno), "state", "UP", "mtu", mtu,
90				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is UP (mtu=%d) but writes are still failing — check the link/driver (install.md §4)", d.name, mtu))
91		}
92	}
```

```go
1	// Command wanbond is the wanbond tunnel daemon. A single binary serves both
2	// roles (edge and concentrator); the role is selected from configuration at
3	// runtime, not by which binary is invoked.
4	package main
5	
6	import (
7		"flag"
8		"fmt"
9		"os"
10		"os/signal"
11		"syscall"
12	
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
14		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/device"
15		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
16	)
17	
18	// version is overridden at build time via -ldflags "-X main.version=...".
19	var version = "dev"
20	
21	func main() {
22		if err := run(os.Args[1:]); err != nil {
23			fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, "wanbond:", err)
24			os.Exit(1)
25		}
26	}
27	
28	// run is the testable entry point: it parses the command line, loads the
29	// configuration, brings the tunnel up for the configured role, and blocks until
30	// a termination signal, then tears the tunnel down.
31	func run(args []string) error {
32		if len(args) == 1 && (args[0] == "version" || args[0] == "--version") {
33			fmt.Println("wanbond", version)
34			return nil
35		}
36	
37		fs := flag.NewFlagSet("wanbond", flag.ContinueOnError)
38		configPath := fs.String("config", "", "path to the TOML configuration file (mode 0600)")
39		if err := fs.Parse(args); err != nil {
40			return err
41		}
42		if *configPath == "" {
43			return fmt.Errorf("--config is required (or `wanbond version`)")
44		}
45	
46		cfg, err := config.Load(*configPath)
47		if err != nil {
48			return err
49		}
50	
51		lg, err := log.New(cfg.Log.Level, os.Stderr)
52		if err != nil {
53			return err
54		}
55		main := lg.Component("main")
56		main.Info("wanbond starting", "version", version, "role", string(cfg.Role))
57		warnUnverifiableWeightedCapacity(main, cfg)
58	
59		tun, err := device.Up(cfg, lg)
60		if err != nil {
61			return err
62		}
63		defer tun.Close()
64	
65		main.Info("tunnel interface up", "interface", tun.Name(), "role", string(cfg.Role))
66	
67		// SIGINT/SIGTERM terminate; SIGHUP reloads the config file to add/remove paths
68		// on the running tunnel (T30). SIGHUP is idiomatic for a daemon config reload and
69		// needs no extra socket or privilege.
70		sig := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
71		signal.Notify(sig, syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM, syscall.SIGHUP)
72		stopped := deviceStopped(tun)
73		for {
74			select {
75			case s := <-sig:
76				if s == syscall.SIGHUP {
77					reloadTunnel(main, tun, *configPath)
78					continue
79				}
80				main.Info("shutting down", "signal", s.String())
81				return nil
82			case <-stopped:
83				// Unrecoverable engine teardown: fail loud so the exit status reflects it
84				// and a supervisor (systemd Restart=on-failure) restarts the daemon.
85				return fmt.Errorf("tunnel device stopped unexpectedly")
86			}
87		}
88	}
89	
90	// warnUnverifiableWeightedCapacity logs the Q52 WARN-arm startup diagnostic (T144)
91	// when cfg's weighted-policy capacity-sanity verdict is UNVERIFIABLE — some or all
92	// paths leave link_bandwidth undeclared, so the aggregate capacity accounting cannot
93	// be checked. It is a NO-OP (not applicable, no WARN) when cfg.WeightedCapacitySane
94	// is nil (the active-backup policy) or SANE-VERIFIED (every path declared). This is
95	// the soft, non-blocking complement to config.Load's T142 hard-fail guard: startup
96	// must NEVER be blocked on an unverifiable declaration, only on a DECLARED path that
97	// actively contradicts the guard (which Load already refused before main ever runs).
98	func warnUnverifiableWeightedCapacity(lg log.Logger, cfg *config.Config) {
99		if cfg.WeightedCapacitySane == nil || *cfg.WeightedCapacitySane {
100			return
101		}
102		declared := 0
103		for _, p := range cfg.Paths {
104			if p.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec > 0 {
105				declared++
106			}
107		}
108		lg.Warn("weighted policy: link_bandwidth capacity is unverifiable",
109			"declared_paths", declared, "total_paths", len(cfg.Paths),
110			"remedy", "declare link_bandwidth on ALL paths so capacity can be checked, or verify per_path_capacity_fps against the BDP sizing in install.md §3a")
111	}
112	
113	// reloadTunnel re-reads and validates the config file and applies its path diff to
114	// the running tunnel. A bad reload (unreadable, insecure-mode, invalid, or a failed
115	// path change) is LOGGED and the running tunnel is left intact — a reload must never
116	// tear down a live session. config.Load performs the fail-fast validation.
117	func reloadTunnel(lg log.Logger, tun *device.Tunnel, configPath string) {
118		cfg, err := config.Load(configPath)
119		if err != nil {
120			lg.Error("config reload rejected; keeping running config", "error", err.Error())
121			return
122		}
123		if err := tun.Reload(cfg); err != nil {
124			lg.Error("config reload failed to apply; tunnel still running", "error", err.Error())
125			return
126		}
127		lg.Info("config reloaded")
128	}
129	
130	// deviceStopped returns a channel that closes when the tunnel device tears down
131	// on its own (an unrecoverable engine error), so run can exit rather than block
132	// on a dead device.
133	func deviceStopped(t *device.Tunnel) <-chan struct{} {
134		done := make(chan struct{})
135		go func() {
136			t.Wait()
137			close(done)
138		}()
139		return done
140	}
```

200		defer g.mu.Unlock()
201		if a.Configured() {

204					"amneziawg-go keeps the magic-header message types in process-global state and resets them to defaults on ANY engine's Close, " +
205					"so a configured engine requires process exclusivity — at most one, with no other engine alongside it (D2)")
206			}
207			g.configLive = true
208			return nil
209		}
210		if g.configLive {
211			return fmt.Errorf("device: refusing to start a second engine while an amnezia-configured engine is live: " +
212				"closing this engine would reset amneziawg-go's process-global message types to defaults under the live amnezia tunnel (D2)")
213		}
214		g.plainLive++
215		return nil

217	
218	// release drops an engine's hold on the guard. A configured engine clears the
219	// single configured slot; a plain engine decrements the live plain count.
220	func (g *amneziaGuard) release(a config.Amnezia) {
221		g.mu.Lock()
222		defer g.mu.Unlock()
223		if a.Configured() {
224			g.configLive = false
225			return
226		}
227		if g.plainLive > 0 {
228			g.plainLive--
229		}
230	}
231	
232	// Up creates the TUN, wires the multipath Bind into the amneziawg engine,
233	// applies the crypto configuration from cfg, and brings the device up. The same
234	// path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI fields cfg carries
235	// (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).
236	func Up(cfg *config.Config, lg log.Logger) (*Tunnel, error) {
237		clg := lg.Component("device")
238	
239		tunDev, err := tun.CreateTUN(defaultTUNName, tunMTU(cfg))
240		if err != nil {
241			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: create TUN: %w", err)
242		}
243		name, err := tunDev.Name()
244		if err != nil {
245			_ = tunDev.Close()
246			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: read TUN name: %w", err)
247		}
248		// Bring the interface administratively UP now (I1): amneziawg-go never does this
249		// itself, and a write to a DOWN TUN yields EIO — a silent-dead-tunnel footgun the
250		// operator previously had to work around with an out-of-band `ip link set up`.
251		// Addressing stays operator-owned; this sets ONLY the IFF_UP flag (see ifUp).
252		if err := ifUp(name); err != nil {
253			_ = tunDev.Close()
254			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: bring interface up: %w", err)
255		}
256		// Apply the TUN persistence policy (I7, Q38) BEFORE the amnezia single-engine
257		// guard in up(): with tun_persist=true the kernel keeps wanbond0 (and its
258		// operator-owned addresses/routes) across a daemon restart; with the default
259		// false it explicitly CLEARS the flag so the device disappears on Close exactly
260		// as before. This composes with the ifUp above and does not touch the reload or
261		// restart-on-failure paths — a reload never re-creates the TUN, and a persistent
262		// device is simply re-adopted by name on the next Up (see setTUNPersist).
263		if err := setTUNPersist(tunDev, cfg.TUNPersist); err != nil {
264			_ = tunDev.Close()
265			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: set TUN persistence: %w", err)
266		}
267		clg.Info("interface up", "interface", name, "tun_persist", cfg.TUNPersist)
268		// The DNS re-resolution transport is the one the (validated) [dns] block selects. It is passed
269		// as a factory (not eagerly constructed) so up() builds it AT MOST ONCE and ONLY when some peer
270		// carries a hostname endpoint spec — a config with zero hostname specs never constructs a
271		// resolver (Q29 inertness). The injected TUN and factory are also the seams device tests drive.
272		return up(cfg, clg, tunDev, name, cfg.DNS.NewResolver)
273	}
274	
275	// resolverFactory builds the DNS resolver used for hostname endpoint re-resolution. It is injected
276	// so a device test can supply a fake; production passes cfg.DNS.NewResolver. up() invokes it AT
277	// MOST ONCE, and ONLY when some peer carries a hostname endpoint spec.
278	type resolverFactory func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error)
279	
280	// up drives the tunnel bring-up over an already-created TUN and an injected resolver factory: it
281	// wires the multipath Bind into the amneziawg engine, performs the bounded initial hostname
282	// resolve (Q30), applies the crypto/endpoint UAPI config built ONLY from resolved entries, brings
283	// the device up, and starts the probe / reconcile / hub-failover / re-resolution loops. Splitting
284	// it out of Up gives device tests a seam to inject a channel TUN and a fake resolver without the
285	// privileged tun.CreateTUN. The same path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI
286	// fields cfg carries (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).
287	func up(cfg *config.Config, clg log.Logger, tunDev tun.Device, name string, newResolver resolverFactory) (*Tunnel, error) {
288		// Wrap the TUN so a write that fails with EIO gets an actionable, rate-limited diagnostic
289		// (naming the interface's link state/MTU and the remedy) alongside the raw errno, instead
290		// of surfacing only as the engine's generic "input/output error" (D39, I3). Every other
291		// Device method is unaffected.
292		tunDev = newDiagnosingTUN(tunDev, name, clg)
293	
294		// Claim the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant (D2) BEFORE IpcSet
295		// assigns amneziawg-go's process-global message-type state. On any failure
296		// below, ok stays false and the deferred release returns the hold; the
297		// successful path transfers the hold to the returned Tunnel.
298		if err := globalAmneziaGuard.acquire(cfg.Amnezia); err != nil {
299			_ = tunDev.Close()
300			return nil, err
301		}
302		ok := false
303		defer func() {
304			if !ok {
305				globalAmneziaGuard.release(cfg.Amnezia)
306			}
307		}()
308	
309		// One random per-boot probe session id, shared by every path AND every configured peer (it
310		// identifies THIS boot, not a path or peer): a peer restart presents a new id that resets
311		// the surviving responder's anti-replay high-water so liveness recovers (T38, D12), and a
312		// runtime-added path (T30) reuses it so its probes join this boot's stream.
313		sessionID, err := telemetry.NewSessionID(rand.Reader)
314		if err != nil {
315			_ = tunDev.Close()
316			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: new probe session id: %w", err)
317		}
318		// PeerIdentities is the SINGLE place the single-peer/multi-peer effective-PSK decision is
319		// made (G4): for a single-peer config every identity's PSK is the top-level psk; for a
320		// multi-peer concentrator each is that peer's own psk. Its order matches cfg.WireGuard.Peers
321		// (and thus uapiConfig's peer render), so the engine peer set and the Bind peer set agree.
322		ids := cfg.PeerIdentities()
323		// The primary peer (peers[0]) drives on-wire liveness through the SAME prober values that
324		// back its scheduler's PathHealth (T37). It is keyed on the FIRST identity's effective psk:
325		// the top-level psk for a single-peer config (byte-identical to pre-G4), or peer 0's own psk
326		// for a multi-peer concentrator.
327		scheduler, probers, newProber, err := buildScheduler(cfg, ids[0].PSK, sessionID, clg)
328		if err != nil {
329			_ = tunDev.Close()

```go
1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
5		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
6	)
7	
8	// MTU accounting for the bonded datapath.
9	//
10	// A tunnelled application packet is wrapped in four nested layers before it hits
11	// the wire, each adding fixed overhead:
12	//
13	//		[ IP | UDP | outer DATA frame | WG transport | inner IP payload ]
14	//		  \___ IPv4UDPOverhead ___/   \_DataOverhead_/\_WGTransportOverhead_/
15	//
16	//	  - IP + UDP: the underlay carrying our outer datagram.
17	//	  - outer DATA frame: the bonding codec's nonce + DATA header (frame.DataOverhead).
18	//	  - WG transport: WireGuard's own data-message header + Poly1305 tag.
19	//
20	// InnerMTU sizes the TUN so a full-MTU inner packet, once wrapped in all four
21	// layers, still fits the path MTU without IP fragmentation. Fragmentation must be
22	// avoided: a fragment lost on a lossy WAN drops the whole datagram, and PMTUD
23	// black holes (ICMP "fragmentation needed" filtered by a middlebox) silently stall
24	// the tunnel. See docs/p1-mtu.md for the derivation and the MSS-clamping guidance
25	// that keeps TCP inside this budget.
26	const (
27		// DefaultPathMTU is the assumed underlay path MTU when none is measured. 1500
28		// is the near-universal Ethernet/most-broadband figure; deployments on links
29		// with a smaller MTU (some LTE/PPPoE uplinks) must lower it.
30		DefaultPathMTU = 1500
31	
32		// IPv4UDPOverhead is the outer IPv4 (20) + UDP (8) header cost. IPv4 is the
33		// conservative default here; an IPv6 underlay costs 20 bytes more (see
34		// IPv6UDPOverhead) so sizing for IPv4 and running over IPv6 only wastes a few
35		// bytes, never fragments.
36		IPv4UDPOverhead = 20 + 8
37	
38		// IPv6UDPOverhead is the outer IPv6 (40) + UDP (8) header cost.
39		IPv6UDPOverhead = 40 + 8
40	
41		// WGTransportOverhead is WireGuard's per-datagram data-message overhead: the
42		// 16-byte transport header (msg type + reserved + receiver index + counter)
43		// plus the 16-byte Poly1305 authentication tag. Amnezia junk PREFIXES add
44		// further variable bytes on top; they are handled as best-effort headroom, not
45		// subtracted here, because they are configurable and per-packet variable.
46		WGTransportOverhead = 16 + 16
47	)
48	
49	// FECParityMTUPenalty is the extra bytes a full-size FEC PARITY frame occupies on the
50	// wire over a DATA frame carrying the same-size inner payload (T24). A parity frame is
51	// nonce + PARITY header + shard payload, where the shard payload is the codec's length
52	// prefix + the FEC outer-seq prefix + the largest coded inner payload; a data frame is
53	// nonce + DATA header + that same inner payload. So the delta is:
54	//
55	//	(ParityOverhead + ShardFramingOverhead + fecSeqPrefixLen) - DataOverhead  ==  5 bytes.
56	//
57	// When FEC is enabled the inner MTU is reduced by this penalty so a full-MTU DATA frame
58	// AND its group's PARITY frame both fit the path MTU — otherwise parity exceeds the
59	// path MTU by exactly this much and IP-fragments or is EMSGSIZE/PMTUD-blackholed, which
60	// silently kills the very redundancy FEC exists to provide on bulk full-size traffic.
61	const FECParityMTUPenalty = frame.ParityOverhead + fec.ShardFramingOverhead + fecSeqPrefixLen - frame.DataOverhead
62	
63	// InnerMTU returns the largest inner (TUN) MTU that avoids IP fragmentation over
64	// an IPv4 underlay of the given path MTU: pathMTU minus the IP+UDP, outer DATA
65	// frame, and WireGuard transport overheads. fecEnabled additionally reserves the
66	// parity-over-data delta so a full-size parity frame also fits (see
67	// FECParityMTUPenalty); pass false when FEC is off to keep the pre-T24 budget.
68	func InnerMTU(pathMTU int, fecEnabled bool) int {
69		return pathMTU - IPv4UDPOverhead - frame.DataOverhead - WGTransportOverhead - fecPenalty(fecEnabled)
70	}
71	
72	// InnerMTU6 is InnerMTU for an IPv6 underlay.
73	func InnerMTU6(pathMTU int, fecEnabled bool) int {
74		return pathMTU - IPv6UDPOverhead - frame.DataOverhead - WGTransportOverhead - fecPenalty(fecEnabled)
75	}
76	
77	// fecPenalty is the inner-MTU reduction to reserve for FEC parity, or 0 when FEC is
78	// off.
79	func fecPenalty(fecEnabled bool) int {
80		if fecEnabled {
81			return FECParityMTUPenalty
82		}
83		return 0
84	}
```
